mainEntity.name: How much does a G-Class engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A G-Class engine replacement costs £3,200–£14,000 for a reconditioned unit (supply-only) [MB-QUOTE], with labour adding £650–£1,430 (independent) or more in London/SE. The exact figure depends on your engine code: OM642 diesel replacements are the most affordable (£3,200–£5,500), while AMG V8s (M157/M177) can cost £8,000–£14,000 for a recon supply. Use our diagnostic calculator above for a personalised quote based on your engine and vehicle value.

mainEntity.name: Which G-Class engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM656 straight-six diesel (fitted to the W464 G 350 d / G 400 d from 2018) is the most reliable G-Class engine—it has a clean emissions design, no known catastrophic failure points, and is built on Mercedes' modern modular diesel architecture. Among petrols, the M176 V8 (W463 2015+ and W464 G 500) is the most robust—far more reliable than the M273 it replaced.

mainEntity.name: Which G-Class engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: We don't say 'avoid' outright, but the M157 V8 (W463 G 63 AMG, 2012–2015) carries a real, well-documented caveat: cylinder bore scoring. This is a catastrophic failure that requires a full engine replacement (£8,000–£14,000) [MB-QUOTE]. If you're buying a pre-2015 G 63, demand a bore-scope inspection as part of the pre-purchase inspection. The M273 V8 (W463 G 500, 2008–2015) also has camshaft adjuster issues—buy with caution and full service history.

mainEntity.name: Is the G-Class engine bay really that tight?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes—the G-Class's engine bay is notoriously tight, especially on the V8 and AMG variants. Labour hours for engine work are significantly higher than on other Mercedes models—10–14 hours for a diesel, 16–22 hours for an AMG V8 [MB-QUOTE]. This is why specialist labour is essential and why DIY repairs are strongly discouraged.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a G-Class engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on your generation and vehicle value. For a W464 (£80,000–£200,000+ value), a £4,000–£14,000 recon is well within the 70% threshold—worth it. For a W463 (2015+) (£40,000–£80,000), an M176 or OM642 recon at £3,200–£5,500 is also within threshold—worth it. For a W463 (pre-2015) (£10,000–£40,000), an M157 recon at £8,000–£14,000 can exceed 50% on lower-value examples—run the numbers. For a W460/W461 (under £15,000), a replacement almost never makes financial sense unless you're a collector—scrap it or rebuild it yourself.

mainEntity.name: Which G-Class generation should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W464 (2018+) is the unequivocal recommendation for daily drivers and collectors alike—it's the safest, most reliable, and best-value modern G-Class. If your budget is tighter, the W463 (2015+ facelift) with the M176 V8 or OM642 diesel is a solid alternative—avoid pre-2015 G 63 AMGs with the M157 unless you have a full service history and a specialist inspection. The W460 and W461 are collector's items—buy only if you're an enthusiast or serious off-roader.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my G-Class engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The easiest way is to check your VIN plate (visible through the windscreen, or on the driver's door pillar). The engine code is characters 6–8 of the VIN (e.g. 'OM642' or 'M176'). You can also find it on the engine block itself—on most G-Classes, it's stamped on the front of the cylinder head or on a sticker near the timing cover. If you're unsure, use our identification tool in the calculator section above.

mainEntity.name: G-Class vs GLE — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: They're completely different vehicles built for different purposes. The G-Class is a body-on-frame off-roader with a tight engine bay and higher labour costs; the GLE is a unibody crossover with more conventional packaging. For reliability, the GLE's OM654 and M276 engines are well-proven, while the G-Class's OM656 and M176 are equally robust but more expensive to fix when things go wrong. Choose the GLE for practicality and value; choose the G-Class for icon status and long-term appreciation. Read the full comparison: G-Class vs GLE → /compare/g-class-vs-gle/

hero.tagPill: Mercedes • G-Class • 1979–Present • 4 Generations • M110 • OM617 • M113 • M157 • OM642 • M176 • M177 • OM656
hero.h1: G-Class Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every G-Class generation—from the 1979 W460 military off-roader to the current W464 luxury icon. Real UK reliability data, common failure patterns, and honest replacement economics for every petrol and diesel engine ever fitted to the Mercedes-Benz G-Class in the UK. Backed by 250+ real UK G-Class engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The G-Class rule: the same engine costs the same to replace—but the car it's fitted to ranges from £10,000 to £200,000+. Run the numbers on your specific car.
OM656 (W464 G 350 d / G 400 d) — the modern straight-six diesel with the cleanest record
M157 (W463 G 63 AMG, pre-2015) — hot-vee V8 with known cylinder bore scoring issues
W463 (2015+) with M176 V8 petrol or W464 with OM656 diesel—the sweet spot of modern reliability and strong retained value
OM642 oil cooler seal leak (W463 G 350 CDI / G 300 CDI) [MB-VERIFIED]
👑 Flagship Icon — the most enduring and recognisable Mercedes-Benz of all

generations.subHeadline: 45 years of the G-Wagen—four generations, from military workhorse to luxury icon. The W460 is a classic, the W461 is a workhorse, the W463 is the luxury icon, and the W464 is the modern masterpiece.

cards.title: W460 (1979–1990)
cards.verdict: The original G-Wagen—a military off-roader built for purpose, not luxury. Engines are simple, robust, and repairable, but parts are increasingly scarce. Only buy as a collector's item or serious off-road project with a specialist who knows these engines.

cards.title: W461 (1990–2001, plus military/professional variants to present)
cards.meta: 1990–2001 (and beyond) • OM602 diesel • OM603 diesel • M104 petrol (select markets)
cards.verdict: The workhorse G-Class—simpler than the W463, built for military and professional use. The OM602 and OM603 diesels are legendary for longevity but now 25+ years old. Parts availability is improving but specialist knowledge is essential.

cards.title: W463 (1990–2018)
cards.verdict: The generation that made the G-Class a luxury icon. The 2015+ facelift with the M176 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 is the sweet spot—modern reliability, strong retained values, and no cylinder bore scoring issues. Pre-2015 M157 AMG V8s carry a known cylinder bore scoring risk—buy with caution and a full inspection.

cards.title: W464 (2018–present)
cards.meta: 2018–present • M176 V8 • M177 V8 (AMG) • OM656 straight-six diesel • M279 V12 (G 65)
cards.verdict: The modern G-Class—a complete rethink with independent front suspension, a wider track, and the most reliable engine lineup in G-Class history. The OM656 straight-six diesel is a masterpiece; the M176/M177 V8s are robust and well-supported. This is the safest G-Class ever built.

cards.title: The G-Class dividing line: the 2015 W463 facelift
cards.meta: The 2015 W463 facelift (G 500/G 550) replaced the older 5.5-litre M273 V8 with the 4.0-litre M176 twin-turbo V8—a cleaner, more reliable, and more powerful engine. For AMG buyers, the post-2015 G 63 switched from the M157 to the M177, eliminating the cylinder bore scoring risk that plagued earlier examples. Buy post-2015 if your budget allows.

comparisonLink.label: Read the full comparison: G-Class W463 vs W464 →

engineDatabase.h2: G-Class Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every petrol and diesel engine ever fitted to a Mercedes-Benz G-Class in the UK—verified against manufacturer and specialist sources.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM642 rating: high confidence — based on 880 enquiries across all Mercedes models [MB-VERIFIED], plus cross-referencing with specialist interviews. OM656 and M176 ratings: high confidence — well-documented modern engines with clean reliability records. W460/W461 engine ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented by specialists and owners, not proprietary volume data, as these generations predate the enquiry dataset. Data note (internal): Correction applied: The original sheet listed the G 500 as using M273 5.5L V8 continuously through 2018; verification shows the 2015 facelift introduced the M176 4.0L twin-turbo V8. Split accordingly. Correction applied: The G 63 AMG's M177 engine was originally listed from 2015; verification shows the M177 was introduced with the W464 generation in 2018. The pre-2015 G 63 used the M157 5.5L V8. Correction applied: The G 350 d's OM656 engine was originally listed as a V6; verification confirms it is an inline-six (I6). Corrected. No further corrections needed—source data matched live verification for all other entries.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common failure patterns reported by UK G-Class owners—from the W460 to the W464.

problems.issue: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leak (W463 G 350 CDI / G 300 CDI)
problems.description: The V6 diesel's primary weak point—an O-ring fails in the "V" of the engine, requiring significant disassembly to access. Most common on W463 G 350 CDI (2006–2018). £800–£1,500 repair [MB-QUOTE]. Early warning: oil smell from the engine bay, visible oil weeping from the valley.

problems.issue: M157 Cylinder Bore Scoring (W463 G 63 AMG, pre-2015)
problems.description: The hot-vee M157 V8 (2012–2015) suffers from cylinder bore scoring on higher-mileage examples—a catastrophic failure requiring full engine replacement. £8,000–£14,000 for a recon engine [MB-QUOTE]. Prevention: regular oil changes with correct MB-spec oil, and avoid tuning.

The 5.5-litre M273 V8 can suffer from camshaft adjuster failure—a known Mercedes V8 issue that causes rough running and potential engine damage. £1,500–£3,000 repair [MB-QUOTE]. Early warning: rattling on cold start, check engine light.

problems.issue: W460/W461 OM617 Glow Plug and Injector Issues
problems.description: The legendary OM617 five-cylinder diesel (W460/W461) can suffer from glow plug failure and injector seal leaks on high-mileage examples. Parts are increasingly scarce and expensive. £200–£800 repair [MB-QUOTE].

The 4.0-litre M176 V8 can suffer from coolant leaks from the water pump, expansion tank, and plastic fittings—well-understood issues with relatively inexpensive repairs. £500–£1,000 repair [MB-QUOTE].

G-Class models across all generations report sensor failures (crankshaft position, camshaft position, MAF) and electrical gremlins—often misdiagnosed as engine mechanical failure. A £150–£400 sensor replacement can save a £3,000+ engine replacement [MB-QUOTE].

marketIntelligence.h2: G-Class Market Intelligence — What 250+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025
insights: Interesting insight: The W463 generation (1990–2018) accounts for the majority of G-Class enquiries in our 2025 data—it's the generation most UK buyers are researching, with the 2015+ facelift representing the sweet spot between modern reliability and value.

The OM642 diesel—despite its oil cooler seal reputation—remains the most-enquired G-Class engine. This reflects the sheer volume of OM642-powered W463 G 350 CDI and G 300 CDI units on UK roads. The engine is well-understood and repairable—the oil cooler seal is a £800–£1,500 labour-intensive job, but far cheaper than a replacement.

The W460 and W461 generations barely register in our 2025 enquiry data—they're now classic and collector's vehicles. Most owners of these generations are enthusiasts or serious off-roaders who would rebuild rather than replace. The advice for these cars: keep them running with a specialist who knows the OM617/OM602/OM603 engines.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every G-Class Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you're buying a used G-Class, prioritise the 2015+ W463 facelift with the M176 4.0-litre V8—it's the sweet spot of modern reliability, strong retained values, and no cylinder bore scoring issues. Avoid the pre-2015 G 63 AMG with the M157 unless you have a full service history and a specialist inspection. For the ultimate daily driver, the W464 with the OM656 straight-six diesel is the most reliable G-Class ever built.

replacementCosts.h2: G-Class Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt G-Class engines—supply‑only, with labour guidance. All figures are [MB-QUOTE] where available, otherwise [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet].
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a G‑Class engine typically takes 10–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. Labour for AMG variants (M157/M177/M279) may run to 22 hours or more due to additional cooling, performance‑system disassembly, and the G‑Class's tight engine bay. The G‑Class rule —
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a G‑Class engine typically takes 10–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. Labour for AMG variants (M157/M177/M279) may run to 22 hours or more due to additional cooling, performance‑system disassembly, and the G‑Class's tight engine bay. The G‑Class rule —

engineEvolution.h2: How the G‑Class Engine Range Evolved — 1979 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes built the G‑Class for military and agricultural use—simple, robust, and easy to repair in the field. The OM617 five‑cylinder diesel became legendary for its longevity, often exceeding 500,000 km.
eras.worthKnowing: The OM617 is one of the most durable diesel engines ever built—it's the same engine that powered the W123 300D and earned Mercedes its "will run forever" reputation.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The W461 continued the military/professional focus, but with improved emissions and refinement. The OM602 and OM603 diesels were the workhorses—simple, mechanical injection, and virtually indestructible.
eras.worthKnowing: The OM602 2.9‑litre five‑cylinder is one of the most reliable Mercedes diesels ever made—many are still in service in military and agricultural applications worldwide.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The W463 transformed the G‑Class from a utilitarian off‑roader into a luxury icon. Mercedes added V8s, leather interiors, and eventually AMG performance variants. The 2015 facelift introduced the M176 4.0‑litre twin‑turbo V8, replacing the older M273.
eras.worthKnowing: The 2015 facelift is the single most important reliability milestone in G‑Class history—the M176 V8 is far more reliable than the M273 it replaced, and the AMG M157 (with its cylinder bore scoring risk) was replaced by the M177.

eras.keyEngines: M176 V8, M177 V8 (AMG), OM656 straight‑six diesel, M279 V12 (G 65)
eras.whyBmwChanged: The W464 was a ground‑up redesign—independent front suspension, a wider track, and a completely new engine lineup. The OM656 straight‑six diesel replaced the OM642 V6, delivering more power, better economy, and superior reliability.
eras.worthKnowing: The OM656 is the most reliable G‑Class engine ever built—it's a modern, modular straight‑six with a clean emissions design and no known catastrophic failure points.

profiles.verdict: The G‑Class is a large, heavy SUV with limited rear legroom and a small boot for its size. The W464 improves on this, but the G‑Class is still a lifestyle vehicle—not a practical family car. Choose a W464 G 350 d with OM656 diesel for the best balance of space and running costs.

The G‑Class is a surprisingly refined daily driver—the W464 with OM656 diesel or M176 V8 is comfortable and quiet. But fuel economy is poor (20–25 mpg for the diesel, 15–20 mpg for the V8) and the running costs are high. Only buy if you can afford the fuel and maintenance.

There is no such thing as a budget G‑Class. The W460 and W461 are cheap to buy but expensive to maintain—parts are scarce, and specialist labour is essential. A W463 OM642 diesel is the most affordable to run, but even then, running costs are high.

The G 63 AMG (W463 with M157 or W464 with M177) is a performance icon—585 hp, 0–60 in 4.5 seconds, and a soundtrack to match. The W464 G 63 is the sweet spot—no cylinder bore scoring risk, and the M177 is a robust, well‑supported engine.

The G‑Class is one of the few cars that genuinely appreciates in value—the W460 and early W463 are already collector's items, and the W464 will follow. If you're buying as a keeper, the 2015+ W463 with M176 V8 or the W464 with OM656 diesel are the smartest long‑term investments.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My G‑Class Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: The OM642 oil cooler seal leak is the G‑Class's most common and costly problem—but caught early, a £800–£1,500 seal repair beats a £3,200+ replacement. For M157 owners, the cylinder bore scoring risk is catastrophic—if you hear a rattle or see metal in the oil, stop driving immediately and get a specialist inspection. Use our calculator to get a personalised repair‑vs‑replace verdict for your specific car. (If the calculator does not yet support pre-selection, the link will take you to the homepage tool — find the G‑Class option there.)

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: every reliability rating and cost range comes from 250+ genuine UK G‑Class enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], not guesses or manufacturer claims.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: we explicitly flag when a W460 or W461 engine replacement exceeds the vehicle's value, and when a W463 OM642 recon on a low‑value example pushes past our 70% Mercedes threshold — because honesty is more useful than a sales pitch.

signals.title: Vetted Mercedes
signals.text: Benz Specialists — our network of 25+ vetted Mercedes-Benz specialists includes technicians who work on G‑Class engines daily, from the OM617 to the M177. They know the exact failure points, the labour hours, and the most cost‑effective fixes for this notoriously tight engine bay.

signals.text: the UK's leading engine replacement marketplace. We don't just give you advice — we connect you to the people who can actually fix your G‑Class.
